title: When Forests Become Mines
text:
Illegal mining activities cleared protected forest in Inner Mongolia.



This case illustrates how ecological degradation often begins with seemingly localized disturbances but rapidly expands through landscape fragmentation.



This investigation changed the way I understood environmental damage.
It was no longer simply about forests.
It became a question about institutions, incentives, and governance.



The first time I realized that environmental damage is rarely ecological alone. …

title: Journey Template
text:
Location · Years


“A short reflection on how this place changed the way I think.”

title: Journey Template
text:
The Story

Before I Arrived
Use this space for a quiet opening that situates the place and the state of mind you brought with you. Keep it reflective and grounded rather than descriptive for its own sake.


What I Observed
Describe the patterns, textures, and rhythms of the place. Focus on what became legible only once you were there.


What Changed
Explain the shift in perception. This is where the place becomes more than a setting and begins to reshape how you think.


What Stayed With Me
Close with a brief reflection on the lasting idea, question, or method that remained with you after leaving.

title: Journey Template
text:
Thinking Across Scales
This is the signature section. Summarize how this place changed your understanding of systems, scale, feedback, or complexity. Keep it clear, concise, and slightly more distilled than the main narrative.

title: Ecology of Ideas
text:
Ecology of Ideas
Status: 🌱 Seed
One sentence
Explore how ecological ideas travel across cultures, generations, and political systems through conversations with overseas Chinese ecologists.
Questions

Why did they become ecologists?
How did China shape their thinking?
How did the U.S. change their thinking?
Which ecological concepts have been reinterpreted in China?
How has ecology become part of national narratives?

Possible outputs

Documentary series
Podcast
Essays
Interactive map
Interview archive

Possible sponsors

ESA
Universities
Foundations

Related
Thinking Across Scales Journey The Ecology of the Mind

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
White Terror of the Qinghai Rhododendrons
Qinghai Province · Summer 2017
Had it not been for the white rhododendrons blanketing the mountainside, the snow lingering on the peaks of the Qilian Range, and the crystalline air of four thousand meters, I might have left with the same emptiness carved into the riverbanks and hollowed out of the mountains themselves.
Policies descended from above.
Cigarette butts accumulated below.
I walked across a mound of illegally excavated gravel, measuring distances that would later become estimates in a damage assessment report.
A local official waited nearby.
Small in stature, weathered by the plateau, and speaking with a thick local accent, he never stopped smoking.
One cigarette after another.
We were standing more than four thousand meters above sea level.
I could not help wondering how his lungs endured what the altitude had already made scarce.
In recent years, environmental policies had arrived one after another.
Ecological redlines.
Ecological compensation.
Green GDP.
Each new directive burned brightly for a day, much like the cigarette between his fingers.
Then the smoke drifted away.
The consequences, however, remained for a lifetime. We climbed higher into the valley.
Snow still rested on the ridges above us.
The shaded slopes were covered by carpets of Qinghai rhododendrons in full bloom.
Their pale blossoms seemed to exist for beauty alone.
Then, around the bend, another quarry appeared.
An entire mountainside had been sliced away.
Standing there, I felt as though something had been cut from me as well.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
The Fall of a Tulip Tree
Guizhou, China · May 2017
It had survived the ice ages of the Quaternary, only to fall beneath the steel teeth of an excavator.
A stand of wild Chinese tulip trees (Liriodendron chinense), an ancient magnolia lineage and a nationally protected species in China, had been cleared to make way for a road leading to a wind farm. Nearby, another protected species, Magnolia officinalis var. biloba, faced an uncertain future.
At first glance, the question seemed obvious: should we save the road or save the trees?
But standing there, I realized that was the wrong question.
Was renewable energy always sustainable? Not necessarily.
Was this simply a conflict between economic development and environmental protection? I no longer believed that either.
Something deeper was unfolding beneath the surface.
I arrived at the site in a vehicle provided by the state-owned wind power company itself.
They were proud of the road we were driving on—it had been built by their company.
As an investigator, however, I could not ignore the irony.
The people leading the investigation were also the people being investigated.
Every destination depended on their guidance. Every conversation happened on their terms.
Long before I reached the forest, I had already begun wondering whether independent observation was even possible.
During every break, I wandered over to talk with the construction workers.
I never saw them as “ordinary laborers.”
If anything, they were the anonymous builders carrying the weight of the country’s ambitions on their shoulders.
I photographed them as they assembled turbines against the mountains.
One worker smiled when I promised to send him the photos so he could show his family.
For a moment, I wondered why he had never taken these pictures himself.
Then the answer came quietly.
By the end of the day, work had already consumed everything.
Survival leaves little room for poetry.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text: Y博士巡护祖国生态系列之内蒙古包头站–非法采矿生态破坏鉴定评估。7个半小时26公里的山路，走的不是辛苦，是心酸。曾经当地领导的发展观是“宁愿呛死也不愿饿死”，不计代价不顾后果的上项目拼指标换来的是什么？死路一条？此路不通

title: 鹅掌楸之殇
text:
5/17/2017 尽管穿越了第四纪冰期的严寒，却无法逃脱挖掘机的铁爪。国家二级保护植物、古老的木兰科被子植物鹅掌楸Chinese tulip tree（别名：马褂木）原生天然林在贵州省某地被铲平，倒在风力发电的铺路石下！同为国家二级保护植物的凹叶厚朴同样深陷困境，前途未知！是要保路还是要护树？不是两难问题，是表面问题。可再生能源的开发是可持续发展？未必。深藏幕后的核心冲突是经济发展和生态保护？在我看来也不是。
我作为一个调查人员和某国营风力发电公司的坐在同一辆车上，他们很自豪，进山里的路就是他们公司修起来的。我坐在这样的车里，四个人，司机，负责接待的办事员，首先觉得这样的被告和裁判没有独立，作为调查人员不能单独调查，去任何地方都要他们带路让我失去了行动自由，也就意味着我是不可能有独立的思考的。这里设计国家制度设计的司法独立，已经不能在这里这个简单的故事里讨论了。作为tech geek， 我最好奇的是选址，为什么要在贵州这个山区，这么偏僻的地方建风电？？这个问题我最费解，怎么看都不是合适的地方。首先就是没有风！风力能达到最低选址要求吗？我就问这个办事人员，风电的费用啊，规模啊，他告诉我的是，现在的建风电场主要是国家补贴！我一听就恼火，我就知道，一方面是作为科学家的hypothsis testing 这个地方达不到风力等级,得到证实。另一方吗就是因为，这个熟悉的故事，国家意志凌驾于生态、民生。因为第一次近距离看风电，我有巨物恐惧症啊，看到工人们在施工拼装的时候，我还是很震惊，每到下车活动手脚的时候都跟民工师傅们交流，我从来不觉得他们是‘下等公民’，而相反，他们是unsung hero! 当然这个解读肯定是美利坚的，在国内的叙事是奉献。我给他们拍照，我想把他们在国家第一线，了无人烟的工地热火朝天的工作场景记录下来，我说要发给他，他很高兴，说要发给家里人。我想为什么问他们自己不拍照吗？他们也不是没有手机。转头一想，我自己有了答案，而是工作已经耗尽了所有，眼前只有材米油盐，而不是诗和远方。更加不会把自己的工作上升到无名英雄而自豪吧。

objectID: garden/capture/guizhou-wechat.html#y博士巡护祖国生态系列之贵州站
href: garden/capture/guizhou-wechat.html#y博士巡护祖国生态系列之贵州站

title: Thinking Across Scales is grounded in several organizing principles:
text:
Thinking Across Scales is grounded in several organizing principles:
• Feedback • Scale • Recursive learning • Resilience • Regenerative systems • Emergence • Modularity

title: Journey Index Page Specification
text:
Design the index page for the Journeys section of my Quarto website.
This is NOT a travel blog.
It is an atlas of places that fundamentally transformed how I think.
The page should communicate curiosity, reflection, exploration, and intellectual growth.
This page serves as the primary navigation page for all Journey articles.

title: Journey Index Page Specification
text:
Design the index page for the Journeys section of my Quarto website.
This is NOT a travel blog.
It is an atlas of places that fundamentally transformed how I think.
The page should communicate curiosity, reflection, exploration, and intellectual growth.
This page serves as the primary navigation page for all Journey articles.

title: About
text:
I am an environmental scientist, writer, and systems thinker.
My work explores how ecological thinking can illuminate not only natural systems, but also organizations, cultures, and the human mind.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
The template organizes the page, but never dictates the narrative.
The English version is not a translation of the Chinese. It is another telling of the same journey.
Every Journey should have one sentence that cannot be forgotten.

title: World Map Component
text:
Create a reusable SVG world map component for a Quarto website.
The map should become the primary navigation interface for the Journeys section.
It should be lightweight.
No JavaScript.
Use inline SVG.
Design Style
Minimal
Editorial
Muted earth tones
Thin coastlines
Lots of whitespace
Do not use Google Maps.
Do not use Leaflet.
Do not use Mapbox.
Locations
Everglades
Yellowstone
Yunnan
Tibet
Maine
Sacramento
Each location should appear as a circular marker.
Each marker should link to its corresponding Journey page.
The SVG should scale responsively.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
You are an expert Quarto website developer, UX designer, and frontend engineer.
Your task is to create a production-ready landing page (index.qmd) for my personal website.
Do not explain your reasoning.
Generate clean, maintainable Quarto Markdown with Bootstrap 5.
Do not use JavaScript.
Do not use external frameworks.
Only generate code.



PROJECT



Website Title:
Thinking Across Scales
Website Theme:
A digital garden documenting how landscape ecology evolved into a broader way of understanding systems, feedback, patterns, dynamics, and scale across science, technology, organizations, and everyday life.
The design should feel like:
• Apple • Notion • Linear • Stripe • Modern academic website
Keywords:
minimal elegant scientific storytelling lots of whitespace responsive timeless



LAYOUT



Create the following sections.
==================================================== 1. HERO ====================================================
Large centered title:
Thinking Across Scales
Subtitle:
How studying landscapes became a way of understanding the world.
Use a full-width hero image.
For now use a placeholder image.
Later I will replace it with my own Everglades photograph.
The hero should occupy about 60–70% of the first screen.
Large typography.
Minimal overlay.
==================================================== 2. INTRODUCTION ====================================================
Below the hero.
Use approximately 120 words.
Text:
I was trained as a landscape ecologist, where the most important lesson was not about landscapes themselves, but about learning to think across scales. Over time, that perspective expanded beyond ecology into computation, organizations, design, and everyday life. This website is an evolving record of that journey.
Centered with generous margins.
==================================================== 3. FEATURE CARDS ====================================================
Create four equal Bootstrap cards.
Card 1
Title
Thinking Across Scales
Description
Ideas about feedback, hierarchy, dynamics, and systems.

Card 2
Title
Journeys
Description
Places that transformed how I think.

Card 3
Title
Computational Thinking
Description
Modern scientific workflows and digital craftsmanship.

Card 4
Title
Writing
Description
Essays, notes, and ongoing reflections.

Each card should contain:
Bootstrap Icon
Title
Description
Simple “Explore →” link
Hover effect using CSS only.
==================================================== 4. FEATURED CONTENT ====================================================
Create two side-by-side highlight panels.
Left
Featured Essay
Title:
Why Scale Matters
Placeholder image
Short description
Button:
Read More →

Right
Latest Journey
Title:
Everglades
Placeholder image
Short description
Button:
View Journey →
==================================================== 5. QUOTE ====================================================
Create a full-width elegant quote section.
Quote:
“I am not interested in whether two systems look alike.
I am interested in whether they are organized by the same principles.”
Large serif typography.
Centered.
Subtle background color.
==================================================== 6. FOOTER ====================================================
Simple footer.
Left
Thinking Across Scales
Center
Built with Quarto
Right
Hosted on GitHub



STYLE



Use Bootstrap 5.
Responsive on desktop and mobile.
Lots of whitespace.
Avoid heavy borders.
Use subtle shadows.
Rounded corners.
Professional typography.
No flashy animations.
No JavaScript.



OUTPUT



Generate:

index.qmd
assets/css/custom.css
Any required YAML.

Use relative paths.
The code should be ready to render immediately in Quarto.
Return only code.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Create a reusable Quarto page template for a Journey.
This is NOT a travel blog.
It is a story about how a place transformed the way I think.
The design should feel calm, spacious, reflective, and editorial.
Use the same visual language as the Landing Page.

Page Structure

Hero Section

Large immersive landscape image
Journey title
Location
Years
One-sentence reflection
Example:
Everglades
Florida, USA
2008–2012
“The landscape where I first learned to think across scales.”


At a Glance

Display as four elegant cards
Location
Years
Themes
Related Research


The Story

Long-form narrative.
Use large readable typography.
Break into sections.
Suggested headings:
Before I Arrived
What I Observed
What Changed
What Stayed With Me


Gallery

Responsive image gallery.
Large images with short captions.


Thinking Across Scales

This is the signature section.
Summarize how this place changed my understanding of systems, scale, feedback, or complexity.
This section should be visually distinguished from the main story.


Related Essays

Display 3 linked essays as cards.

Design Principles
Minimal
Editorial
Elegant
Lots of whitespace
No unnecessary decorations
Responsive
Use Bootstrap Grid
Do not invent placeholder content beyond demonstrating layout.
Focus on creating a reusable template rather than a finished article.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
The Mapbox map is working correctly.
Please improve the implementation without changing the overall architecture.
Current implementation
However, several improvements are needed.

title: Thinking Across Scales
section: 1. Automatically fit the map to all locations
text:
1. Automatically fit the map to all locations
Remove the fixed
center: [...]
zoom: ...
Instead, after all markers are created:

Create a mapboxgl.LngLatBounds object.
Extend the bounds with every location.
Call map.fitBounds(bounds).

Use approximately
padding: 80
maxZoom: 2.8
This ensures the map automatically adjusts whenever new places are added.
Do not hard-code the map center.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
2. Improve the default world view
The initial map should display North America and East Asia together.
The current view is centered too far over the Indian Ocean.
The map should feel like a global atlas.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
3. Improve marker styling
Replace the default black markers with a cleaner editorial style.
Use small circular markers.
White fill.
Dark border.
Subtle hover animation.
Markers should feel elegant rather than like Google Maps.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
4. Improve popups
Current popup only displays title and summary.
Instead display
Journey title
Region
Summary
A small “Read Journey →” link
Use clean typography.
Rounded popup.
Maximum width around 260px.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
5. Better map interaction
Disable
rotation
pitch
double-click zoom
Enable
mouse wheel zoom
drag
touch navigation
The interaction should feel calm.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
6. Remove unnecessary UI
Hide
Mapbox attribution (if legally permitted)
Compass
Rotation controls
Keep the interface minimal.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
7. Improve code organization
Split the implementation into reusable functions.
Example
initializeMap()
loadPlaces()
createMarkers()
fitMapToPlaces()
The code should become modular and easier to maintain.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
8. Future compatibility
Keep the implementation compatible with future additions such as
Timeline
Journey filtering
Marker categories
Animated routes
Do not implement these features now.
Simply organize the code so future expansion is straightforward.

Do not redesign the page.
Only improve the Mapbox component.
The goal is to create a reusable World Map component for the entire website.

title: Journey Pipeline
text:
Transform raw travel materials into a published Journey page while preserving the author’s voice. Preserve the author’s narrative voice. Never replace a vivid scene with a structural heading. Structure should guide the writing, not become visible to the reader.





garden/capture/.md
Photos (optional)





Step 1
Read the capture file.
Do not rewrite immediately.
Identify

timeline
important scenes
turning points
recurring themes


Step 2
Produce a curated Journey Note.
Requirements

preserve my voice
remove repetition
do not invent facts
keep chronology
highlight insights

Save as
garden/notes/.md

Step 3
Review manually.
Add reflections that only I can write.

Step 4
Generate
journeys/.qmd
using
journeys/journey-template.qmd

Step 5
Link images.
Export only web-sized images.

Output
A published Journey page.

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Tone
• thoughtful • reflective • interdisciplinary
Avoid
• buzzwords • clickbait • overly academic language

title: 青海杜鹃的白色恐怖
text:
如果不是满山盛开的白色青海杜鹃，积雪祁连山山顶下开阔的草原，清冽的高海拔空气，我可能会随着被挖坑河滩和被掘空的山体而心中空空。上边的政策，下边的烟头。
驱车来到河边，我站在非法采石堆成的小山包那里测量距离，这样回去可以跟上司汇报，估算项目的费用。来接待我的办事员站在不远处，小个头， 干瘦，肤色也是典型的高原色，应该是少数名族小伙，本地口音很重。手里的烟头自打我测量起就没有停过，一根儿接着一根儿。这可是海拔4000多的高原啦，肺能受得了吗？看着他碾碎在河滩边的一地烟头，我不禁感概万千：近年来，政府的红头文件三天两头发，前有生态红线，后有生态损害赔偿，中间还有绿色GDP，每一个文件都好似他手里的那根浸满尼古丁的烟，兴奋一天，结果一溜烟儿，后果是一辈子，老百姓的一生。
河边的石矿看完，他领着我往山上走去。这是个河谷，两边的山顶还有积雪，走上山顶可以俯瞰整个河谷。山坡背阴面是地毯版的青海杜鹃，在这个季节盛开，乳白色的大花心中只有对风景的向往，但真正来到半山腰，另一个非法采石的地点，看着一整个巨大的山体被削去一刀，我的心也被割去了一块。

title: 青海杜鹃的白色恐怖
text:
如果不是满山盛开的白色青海杜鹃，积雪祁连山山顶下开阔的草原，清冽的高海拔空气，我可能会随着被挖坑河滩和被掘空的山体而心中空空。上边的政策，下边的烟头。
驱车来到河边，我站在非法采石堆成的小山包那里测量距离，这样回去可以跟上司汇报，估算项目的费用。来接待我的办事员站在不远处，小个头， 干瘦，肤色也是典型的高原色，应该是少数名族小伙，本地口音很重。手里的烟头自打我测量起就没有停过，一根儿接着一根儿。这可是海拔4000多的高原啦，肺能受得了吗？看着他碾碎在河滩边的一地烟头，我不禁感概万千：近年来，政府的红头文件三天两头发，前有生态红线，后有生态损害赔偿，中间还有绿色GDP，每一个文件都好似他手里的那根浸满尼古丁的烟，兴奋一天，结果一溜烟儿，后果是一辈子，老百姓的一生。
河边的石矿看完，他领着我往山上走去。这是个河谷，两边的山顶还有积雪，走上山顶可以俯瞰整个河谷。山坡背阴面是地毯版的青海杜鹃，在这个季节盛开，乳白色的大花心中只有对风景的向往，但真正来到半山腰，另一个非法采石的地点，看着一整个巨大的山体被削去一刀，我的心也被割去了一块。

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Working title
Ecological Accounting: Thinking about Ecological Compensation and Natural Resource Damage Assessment as Two Temporal Forms of the Same Theory. Core idea
Ecological payment
↓
Pay before degradation.
Ecological damage assessment
↓
Pay after degradation.
Both are attempts to assign value to ecosystem services across time. Status
🌱 Seed
Natural resource damage assessment and ecological compensation are not separate policies, but two complementary ways of assigning value to ecosystems across time.
Evidence
Guizhou Hainan …
Questions
How did this framework evolve? How is it interpreted differently in China and elsewhere?

title: Thinking Across Scales
text:
Thinking Across Scales
How studying landscapes became a way of understanding the world.




I was trained as a landscape ecologist, where the most important lesson was not about landscapes themselves, but about learning to think across scales. Over time, that perspective expanded beyond ecology into computation, organizations, design, and everyday life. This website is an evolving record of that journey.







⟲

Ideas about feedback, hierarchy, dynamics, and systems.
Explore →

Places that transformed how I think.

Modern scientific workflows and digital craftsmanship.

✍️


Writing
Essays, notes, and ongoing reflections.
Explore →

Why Scale Matters
An exploration of scale as a guiding principle for science, design, and systems thinking.
Read More →

“I am not interested in whether two systems look alike. I am interested in whether they are organized by the same principles.”

title: The Fall of a Tulip Tree
text: Guizhou, China · May 2017

title: The Fall of a Tulip Tree
text:
Overview
It had survived the ice ages of the Quaternary, only to fall beneath the steel teeth of an excavator.
A stand of wild Chinese tulip trees (Liriodendron chinense), an ancient magnolia lineage and a nationally protected species in China, had been cleared to make way for a road leading to a wind farm. Nearby, another protected species, Magnolia officinalis var. biloba, faced an uncertain future.

title: The Fall of a Tulip Tree
text:
The Story
At first glance, the question seemed obvious: should we save the road or save the trees?
But standing there, I realized that was the wrong question.
Was renewable energy always sustainable? Not necessarily.
Was this simply a conflict between economic development and environmental protection? I no longer believed that either.
Something deeper was unfolding beneath the surface.
I arrived at the site in a vehicle provided by the state-owned wind power company itself.
They were proud of the road we were driving on—it had been built by their company.
As an investigator, however, I could not ignore the irony.
The people leading the investigation were also the people being investigated.
Every destination depended on their guidance. Every conversation happened on their terms.
Long before I reached the forest, I had already begun wondering whether independent observation was even possible.
During every break, I wandered over to talk with the construction workers.
I never saw them as “ordinary laborers.”
If anything, they were the anonymous builders carrying the weight of the country’s ambitions on their shoulders.
I photographed them as they assembled turbines against the mountains.
One worker smiled when I promised to send him the photos so he could show his family.
For a moment, I wondered why he had never taken these pictures himself.
Then the answer came quietly.
By the end of the day, work had already consumed everything.
Survival leaves little room for poetry.

title: The Fall of a Tulip Tree
text:
Reflection
Survival leaves little room for poetry.

title: Journeys
text:
Places that reshaped how I see the world.
Some places taught me ecology. Some taught me systems. Some taught me computation. Some revealed entirely new ways of seeing. Together they became the foundation of Thinking Across Scales.
